title: My Dog Ate Daffodil Bulbs: Veterinary Triage & Toxicity Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate daffodil bulbs, it's a medium-level emergency. Learn about lycorine toxicity symptoms and why you should call your vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Ingesting daffodil bulbs is a medium urgency emergency that causes severe gastrointestinal distress and requires immediate veterinary attention to prevent serious heart or blood pressure issues.

What happens if my dog eats a daffodil bulb?

Daffodils (Narcissus species) contain several toxic compounds, most notably lycorine, an alkaloid that triggers severe gastrointestinal distress. While the flowers and leaves are toxic, the highest concentration of these toxins is found in the bulb itself. If your dog has dug up and consumed these bulbs, they have ingested the most potent part of the plant, which can irritate the lining of the mouth and stomach.

Is eating a daffodil bulb an emergency for my dog?

  • Ingestion of daffodil bulbs is classified as a Medium Urgency situation that requires prompt treatment to avoid significant pain and illness.
  • Contact your veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline immediately for professional guidance.
  • Monitor your dog for common symptoms including intense vomiting, drooling, diarrhea, and abdominal pain.
  • Be aware that in large quantities or in small dogs, ingestion can lead to severe issues like low blood pressure, tremors, or cardiac arrhythmias.

How does taking a photo of the plant help with my dog's treatment?

  • Safely take a clear photo of the plant, any remaining bulb fragments, or the packaging if the bulbs were recently purchased.
  • Provide this photo to your veterinary team to help them confirm the species and estimate the potential dose of toxin received.
  • Visual evidence helps the vet determine the best course of treatment, such as whether it is safe to induce vomiting or if your dog needs immediate intravenous fluid therapy and monitoring.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Daffodil bulbs contain phenanthridine alkaloids (lycorine, tazettine) which can cause anticholinergic-type effects. Ingestion can result in vomiting, excess salivation, bloody diarrhea (leading to dehydration and electrolyte imbalance), seizures, cardiac function changes, and dermatitis. Treatment requires aggressive supportive and symptomatic therapy with extended monitoring.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3119)

faqs.question: How long does it take for symptoms of daffodil poisoning to appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms of daffodil toxicity usually begin within 15 minutes to a few hours after ingestion.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No, do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian. The toxins can be very irritating, and doing so improperly can lead to aspiration or further esophageal damage.

faqs.question: What is the typical treatment for a dog that ate daffodil bulbs?
faqs.answer: Veterinary treatment often includes decontamination (like gastric lavage or activated charcoal), intravenous fluids to prevent dehydration, and medications to control vomiting and protect the stomach lining.
